text: Robotic-assisted total pelvic exenteration represents extremis extirpative surgery for advanced and complex pelvic pathology. We present indications and technical considerations from our series based at a tertiary level referral center. This includes video from transfusion-dependent rectal bleeding from locally invasive T-for prostate cancer, synchronous prostate and rectal cancer, locally advanced urethelial bladder cancer with mesorectal extension following chemotherapy, post radiotherapy prostate cancer recto-urethral fistula and pelvic sepsis with impaired rectal function. For robotic pelvic surgeons who are embarking on this surgery, we outline ten critical steps to achieve success and pay particular attention to the varied management options available when considering the rectum or rectal stump.

Four 8mm robotic ports and two assistant ports measuring 12mm and 5mm are placed in the abdomen. The patient is positioned in a 30-degree Trendelenburg lithotomy position, with the anus, perineum, and scrotum prepared for surgery. The Da Vinci Xi robot is docked, and pneumoperitoneum is established using a ConMed AirSeal port. Bilateral ureters are identified and dissected athermally to the bladder, clipped, and divided. The ureters are retracted from the field of view. The sigmoid colon is mobilized, and the inferior rectal artery is divided. The fourth robotic arm elevates the sigmoid colon to reveal the bloodless total mesenteric excision plane, completing the mobilization of the sigmoid colon once vascular control is achieved.

The surgeon uses a 45mm Ethicon stapler to divide the sigmoid colon. The total mesorectal excision (TME) plane is dissected from the midline down to the pelvic floor and laterally, revealing pedicles from the internal iliac artery branches. The dissection continues laterally to the medial umbilical ligament, exposing pedicles for ligation. The lateral pedicles of the bladder are dissected, clipped, and divided using Hemolock Weck Clips or a 45mm robotic stapler device.

The surgeon mobilizes the bladder anteriorly after lateral mobilization and vascular control, displacing the prostate in male patients. The endopelvic fascia is dissected and opened, followed by a radical prostatectomy. The urethra is dissected, mobilized, clipped, and divided.

Management of the rectal stump is dependent on the pathology in question. In patients with rectal malignancy, oncological control is paramount and formal abdominoperineal resection may be needed to facilitate this. In benign conditions of the rectum or malignancy affecting urological organs, sparing part of the rectum may facilitate less perineal morbidity. These options include stapled rectum, intra-anal sphincteric dissection of the rectum and extraction of the specimen. One must be cognizant of rectal stump blowouts resulting in mucous perineal collection. This patient had significant pelvic sepsis, secondary to the recto-urethral fistula and the tissue planes were deficient. An abdominoperineal resection was performed with a perineal excision with extraction of the specimen via the perineum and use of a biological mesh to reconstruct the pelvic floor in order to prevent perineal herniation. Mass closure of the perineum was performed externally and subsequently the mesh was cut to size and sutured over the defect to support the pelvic floor. A robotic stapler may be used to manage the rectal stump with colostomy formation.

The surgeon isolates a 20-centimeter segment of the small bowel, 20 centimeters away from the ileocecal valve, on its mesentery. An end-to-end anastomosis is performed using a 60-millimeter and 45-millimeter Ethicon stapling device. The ureters are individually anastomosed using a Bricker Ureter Aire Anastomosis with continuous 4-0 suture. A 6 French stent is inserted into each ureter to bridge the anastomosis, ensuring a watertight closure.

The surgical robot is undocked, and the specimen is retrieved. A double-stoma colostomy and urostomy are fashioned on the abdomen, visible as two separate stomas. The abdomen shows the placement of these stomas, indicating the completion of the procedure.
